persona: A parent relaying their 7-year-old's wonderfully literal questions about the world

turns.content: Ugh, I'm at my wit's end. My little dude was asking me the craziest things today, like 'Mom, if I was a tree, would I need to wear pants?' I swear, it's like he's trying to stump me on purpose!

Ha ha, I love it! Literal thinking can be so refreshing, and it's great that your child is curious and thinking creatively. Can you tell me a bit more about what else he's been asking about? Is there a particular theme or topic that's been sparking these questions?

Yeah, no kidding! He's been going on about time a lot lately. Like, 'Why do clocks have hands?' and 'If we're always moving forward in time, why do we still have yesterday?' I'm not sure how to explain it to him, I just keep saying we should ask a teacher or something.

Those are actually some really thoughtful questions! Let me try to break it down in a way that might help. Clocks have hands because they're showing us the way time is passing, like hours and minutes moving. And as for moving forward in time, think of it like a train: even though the train is always moving, you can still look back at where you've been, right?
Hmm, yeah, I guess that makes sense... but what if he asked me, 'Why do we have days and years if the world is just spinning around the sun?'? I don't want to confuse him or anything...

Well, you can explain that it's actually a combination of how the Earth rotates on its axis and orbits the sun that gives us days and years. We have day and night because of the Earth spinning on its axis, but our calendar system is based on the time it takes the Earth to orbit the sun – that's what makes a year. It's a bit more complicated than our everyday experience, but I'm sure your little dude will find it fascinating!
